skill.name: stripe-integration
skill.description: Implement Stripe payment processing patterns for checkout, subscriptions, webhooks, refunds, and customer payment methods.
skill.summary: Guides Stripe checkout, subscription, webhook, refund, and customer billing implementation.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static shell-execution, weak-crypto, and reconnaissance alerts are false positives caused by Markdown code fences, inline event names, and normal Stripe examples. Real residual risk remains because the skill includes copyable hardcoded secret placeholders and an incomplete manual webhook signature example that could be misused in production.

medium_findings.title: Copyable Hardcoded Stripe Secret Placeholders
medium_findings.description: Examples assign Stripe API and webhook secrets directly in code. The values are placeholders, not real credentials, but the pattern may lead users to hardcode production secrets instead of using secret storage or environment configuration.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are direct assignments of Stripe secret placeholders in executable examples. No real secret is exposed, so the risk is guidance quality and unsafe copy-paste behavior.

medium_findings.title: Incomplete Manual Stripe Webhook Verification Example
medium_findings.description: The manual signature example computes an HMAC over only the payload and compares it with the supplied signature. Stripe webhook verification normally requires validating the signed timestamp payload format, so copying this helper could weaken webhook validation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code shown is semantically incomplete for Stripe webhook signatures. The same file also shows the safer Stripe construct_event helper, which lowers confidence that malicious intent exists.

low_findings.title: Static Shell Execution Alerts Are Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution locations are Markdown inline code or fenced code blocks containing Stripe event names and examples. No evidence found of executable shell commands in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows the backticks are Markdown formatting and Python or JavaScript examples. There is no script file or command launcher present.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Alerts Are Documentation Placeholders
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are example success, cancel, image, and return URLs for Stripe flows. They are expected configuration examples and do not point to a suspicious collection endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are clearly placeholders such as yourdomain.com and example.com. They are not used by the skill itself for background network access.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alert at the metadata description and the reconnaissance alerts in payment examples do not correspond to malicious collection or host inspection behavior. No evidence found of system probing or weak hash use for sensitive data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are normal payment handling text or field assignments. The only visible hash example uses HMAC SHA-256, which is not a weak algorithm in this context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secrets Shown as Inline Assignments
dangerous_patterns.description: Stripe API and webhook secret placeholders are shown as direct source assignments. This is a risky documentation pattern for payment integrations even when placeholder values are used.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code examples visibly place secret-like values in source. The issue is confirmed as a pattern, but not as credential leakage.

dangerous_patterns.title: Custom Webhook Signature Logic
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes custom HMAC webhook verification logic. Payment webhook verification should rely on Stripe supported helpers unless the full Stripe signing scheme is implemented.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The helper omits key parts of Stripe signature verification. It appears to be an educational example, not malicious code.

content.value_statement: Stripe integrations require secure payment, subscription, and webhook handling. This skill gives practical patterns for implementing checkout, billing, refunds, and customer payment methods.
actual_capabilities: Explains hosted Checkout, Payment Intents, Setup Intents, and subscription flows.
Provides webhook handling patterns for payment, invoice, and subscription events.
Shows customer creation, payment method attachment, refunds, and dispute evidence updates.
Documents testing patterns with Stripe test cards and test payment methods.
Highlights PCI, SCA, idempotency, metadata, and retry considerations.
limitations: The referenced resource and asset files are not included in this package.
Examples use placeholders and must be adapted before production use.
The skill does not execute Stripe API calls or verify live account configuration.
It does not replace Stripe documentation, legal review, or PCI compliance review.

use_cases.title: Add Checkout to a SaaS App
use_cases.description: Create hosted checkout and customer portal flows for plans, upgrades, and cancellations.

use_cases.title: Build Reliable Webhook Processing
use_cases.description: Map Stripe events to order fulfillment, subscription access, retries, and idempotent processing.

use_cases.title: Plan Billing Operations
use_cases.description: Review refund, dispute, customer, and payment method workflows before implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Checkout Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this Stripe skill to outline a hosted Checkout flow for a one-time product purchase. Include backend steps, frontend redirects, required configuration, and testing notes.

prompt_templates.title: Design Subscription Billing
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this Stripe skill to design subscription billing for monthly and annual plans. Include customer creation, price IDs, payment confirmation, portal access, and webhook events.

prompt_templates.title: Review Webhook Security
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced security review for payment events.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this Stripe skill to review my webhook handling design. Focus on signature verification, idempotency, event ordering, retries, and safe fulfillment decisions.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Full Billing Migration
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced implementation planning across payment flows.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this Stripe skill to create an implementation plan for migrating from manual invoices to Stripe Checkout, subscriptions, customer portal, refunds, and monitoring.

output_examples.input: Plan hosted Checkout for a paid course.
output: Backend creates the session with product, amount, success URL, and cancel URL.
Frontend redirects to Stripe and waits for webhook-confirmed fulfillment.
Testing covers successful payment, declined card, cancellation, and duplicate webhook delivery.
Verify Stripe signatures before parsing event actions.
Store processed event IDs to prevent duplicate fulfillment.
Handle payment success, payment failure, subscription updates, and subscription deletion.
Use customer and subscription identifiers to update internal access records.

output_examples.input: Prepare refund and dispute handling requirements.
output: Capture payment intent or charge identifiers in your order records.
Support full and partial refunds with reason tracking.
Collect customer communication and shipping evidence for disputes.
Log Stripe errors without exposing secret keys or payment data.

best_practices: Store Stripe API keys and webhook secrets in managed secrets or environment configuration.
Use Stripe hosted or supported client components to reduce PCI scope.
Treat webhooks as the source of truth for fulfillment and subscription access changes.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode production Stripe keys or webhook secrets in source files.
Do not fulfill orders only from client-side redirect success pages.
Do not implement custom webhook signature checks unless they exactly match Stripe requirements.

faq.question: Does this skill process payments by itself?
faq.answer: No. It provides implementation guidance and examples for building Stripe payment flows in your application.

faq.question: Can it help with subscriptions?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers subscription creation, customer portal sessions, invoices, and subscription webhook events.

faq.question: Does it include production-ready files?
faq.answer: No. The package only includes SKILL.md, although it references resources and assets that are not present.

faq.question: How should Stripe secrets be stored?
faq.answer: Use a secret manager or environment configuration. Do not commit live API keys or webhook secrets.

faq.question: Is the skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: It has medium risk. The content is useful, but examples should warn users against hardcoded secrets and incomplete custom webhook verification.

faq.question: Does it replace Stripe documentation?
faq.answer: No. Use it as a planning aid and verify implementation details against current Stripe documentation.
